metadata.title: ICH Stability and Pharmaceutical Development Rules (Q1A/C/D, Q8, Q12)

rules.rule_text: For drug substances stored at ambient conditions (general case), long-term stability studies must be conducted at 25°C ± 2°C/60% RH ± 5% RH OR 30°C ± 2°C/65% RH ± 5% RH, with a minimum of 12 months data at submission.
rules.source_quote: Long term*: 25°C ± 2°C/60% RH ± 5% RH or 30°C ± 2°C/65% RH ± 5% RH — Minimum time period covered by data at submission: 12 months
rules.exceptions: Applicant decides whether to use 25°C/60%RH or 30°C/65%RH for long-term storage.

rules.rule_text: For drug substances stored at ambient conditions (general case), intermediate stability studies must be conducted at 30°C ± 2°C/65% RH ± 5% RH, with a minimum of 6 months data at submission. Intermediate condition is only required when long-term is at 25°C/60%RH.
rules.source_quote: Intermediate**: 30°C ± 2°C/65% RH ± 5% RH — 6 months. **If 30°C ± 2°C/65% RH ± 5% RH is the long-term condition, there is no intermediate condition.
rules.exceptions: No intermediate condition required if 30°C/65%RH is the long-term condition.

rules.rule_text: For drug substances stored at ambient conditions (general case), accelerated stability studies must be conducted at 40°C ± 2°C/75% RH ± 5% RH, with a minimum of 6 months data at submission.
rules.source_quote: Accelerated: 40°C ± 2°C/75% RH ± 5% RH — 6 months

rules.rule_text: Drug substances intended for refrigerator storage must be tested long-term at 5°C ± 3°C (12 months minimum at submission) and accelerated at 25°C ± 2°C/60% RH ± 5% RH (6 months minimum).
rules.source_section: Section 2.1.7.2 Drug substances intended for storage in a refrigerator
rules.source_quote: Long term: 5°C ± 3°C — 12 months. Accelerated: 25°C ± 2°C/60% RH ± 5% RH — 6 months

rules.rule_text: Drug substances intended for freezer storage must be tested long-term at -20°C ± 5°C with a minimum of 12 months data at submission. Re-test period must be based on real-time data.
rules.source_section: Section 2.1.7.3 Drug substances intended for storage in a freezer
rules.source_quote: Long term: -20°C ± 5°C — 12 months. For drug substances intended for storage in a freezer, the re-test period should be based on the real time data obtained at the long term storage condition.
rules.exceptions: No accelerated condition; test single batch at elevated temperature (e.g., 5°C ± 3°C or 25°C ± 2°C) for excursion assessment.

rules.rule_text: Drug substances intended for storage below -20°C must be treated on a case-by-case basis.
rules.source_section: Section 2.1.7.4 Drug substances intended for storage below -20°C
rules.source_quote: Drug substances intended for storage below -20°C should be treated on a case-by-case basis.

rules.rule_text: Data from formal stability studies must be provided on at least three primary batches of the drug substance. Batches must be manufactured to a minimum of pilot scale by the same synthetic route and method of manufacture.
rules.source_quote: Data from formal stability studies should be provided on at least three primary batches of the drug substance. The batches should be manufactured to a minimum of pilot scale by the same synthetic route as, and using a method of manufacture and procedure that simulates the final process to be used for, production batches.
rules.exceptions: Other supporting data can be provided.

rules.rule_text: For drug substance long-term studies with a proposed re-test period of at least 12 months, testing frequency must be every 3 months over the first year, every 6 months over the second year, and annually thereafter.
rules.source_quote: For drug substances with a proposed re-test period of at least 12 months, the frequency of testing at the long term storage condition should normally be every 3 months over the first year, every 6 months over the second year, and annually thereafter through the proposed re-test period.

rules.rule_text: For drug substance accelerated storage studies, a minimum of three time points including initial and final (e.g., 0, 3, and 6 months) from a 6-month study is required.
rules.source_quote: At the accelerated storage condition, a minimum of three time points, including the initial and final time points (e.g., 0, 3, and 6 months), from a 6-month study is recommended.
rules.exceptions: If results are likely to approach significant change criteria, increased testing or a fourth time point should be added.

rules.rule_text: When intermediate storage condition testing is triggered by significant change at accelerated conditions, a minimum of four time points (e.g., 0, 6, 9, 12 months) from a 12-month study is required.
rules.source_quote: When testing at the intermediate storage condition is called for as a result of significant change at the accelerated storage condition, a minimum of four time points, including the initial and final time points (e.g., 0, 6, 9, 12 months), from a 12-month study is recommended.

rules.rule_text: Stress testing of the drug substance must include the effect of temperatures in 10°C increments above accelerated testing (e.g., 50°C, 60°C), humidity (e.g., 75% RH or greater), oxidation, and photolysis. Hydrolysis across a wide pH range must also be evaluated when in solution or suspension.
rules.source_quote: It should include the effect of temperatures (in 10°C increments (e.g., 50°C, 60°C, etc.) above that for accelerated testing), humidity (e.g., 75% RH or greater) where appropriate, oxidation, and photolysis on the drug substance. The testing should also evaluate the susceptibility of the drug substance to hydrolysis across a wide range of pH values when in solution or suspension.
rules.exceptions: Stress testing is carried out on a single batch. Not necessary to examine certain degradation products if not formed under accelerated or long-term storage.

rules.rule_text: Photostability testing must be an integral part of stress testing for drug substances. Standard conditions are described in ICH Q1B.
rules.source_quote: Photostability testing should be an integral part of stress testing. The standard conditions for photostability testing are described in ICH Q1B.

rules.rule_text: Stability studies for drug substances must be conducted on the drug substance packaged in a container closure system that is the same as or simulates the packaging proposed for storage and distribution.
rules.source_quote: The stability studies should be conducted on the drug substance packaged in a container closure system that is the same as or simulates the packaging proposed for storage and distribution.

rules.rule_text: Significant change for a drug substance is defined as failure to meet its specification. If significant change occurs at any time during 6 months of accelerated testing and long-term is at 25°C/60%RH, intermediate testing must be conducted.
rules.source_quote: "Significant change" for a drug substance is defined as failure to meet its specification. If long-term studies are conducted at 25°C ± 2°C/60% RH ± 5% RH and "significant change" occurs at any time during 6 months' testing at the accelerated storage condition, additional testing at the intermediate storage condition should be conducted and evaluated against significant change criteria.

rules.rule_text: When long-term data on primary batches at submission do not cover the proposed re-test period, a commitment must be made to continue stability studies post-approval. If fewer than three production batches are included, additional batches must be added to reach a total of at least three.
rules.source_quote: If the submission includes data from stability studies on fewer than three production batches, a commitment should be made to continue these studies through the proposed re-test period and to place additional production batches, to a total of at least three, on long term stability studies through the proposed re-test period.
rules.exceptions: Post-approval commitment unnecessary if submission includes long-term data from three production batches covering the proposed re-test period.

rules.rule_text: A storage statement must be established for drug substance labeling based on stability evaluation. Terms such as 'ambient conditions' or 'room temperature' must be avoided. A re-test date should be displayed on the container label if appropriate.
rules.source_quote: A storage statement should be established for the labeling in accordance with relevant national/regional requirements. The statement should be based on the stability evaluation of the drug substance. Terms such as "ambient conditions" or "room temperature" should be avoided.

rules.rule_text: Data from formal stability studies for drug products must be provided on at least three primary batches. Two of three batches must be at least pilot scale; the third can be smaller if representative of critical manufacturing steps.
rules.source_quote: Data from stability studies should be provided on at least three primary batches of the drug product. Two of the three batches should be at least pilot scale batches and the third one can be smaller, if justified.
rules.exceptions: Third batch can be smaller if justified.

rules.rule_text: For drug product long-term studies with a proposed shelf life of at least 12 months, testing frequency must be every 3 months over the first year, every 6 months over the second year, and annually thereafter through the proposed shelf life.
rules.source_quote: For products with a proposed shelf life of at least 12 months, the frequency of testing at the long term storage condition should normally be every 3 months over the first year, every 6 months over the second year, and annually thereafter through the proposed shelf life.

rules.rule_text: For drug products stored at ambient conditions (general case), long-term studies must be conducted at 25°C ± 2°C/60% RH ± 5% RH OR 30°C ± 2°C/65% RH ± 5% RH with a minimum of 12 months data at submission.
rules.source_quote: Long term*: 25°C ± 2°C/60% RH ± 5% RH or 30°C ± 2°C/65% RH ± 5% RH — 12 months
rules.exceptions: Applicant decides which long-term condition to use.

rules.rule_text: For drug products stored at ambient conditions (general case), intermediate studies must be conducted at 30°C ± 2°C/65% RH ± 5% RH with a minimum of 6 months data at submission, only when long-term is at 25°C/60%RH.
rules.source_quote: Intermediate**: 30°C ± 2°C/65% RH ± 5% RH — 6 months
rules.exceptions: No intermediate condition if 30°C/65%RH is the long-term condition.

rules.rule_text: For drug products stored at ambient conditions (general case), accelerated studies must be conducted at 40°C ± 2°C/75% RH ± 5% RH with a minimum of 6 months data at submission.
rules.source_quote: Accelerated: 40°C ± 2°C/75% RH ± 5% RH — 6 months

rules.rule_text: Significant change for a drug product includes: (1) 5% change in assay from initial value or failure of biological/immunological potency criteria; (2) any degradation product exceeding its acceptance criterion; (3) failure to meet acceptance criteria for appearance, physical attributes, functionality tests; (4) failure of pH criterion; (5) failure of dissolution criterion for 12 dosage units.
rules.source_quote: In general, "significant change" for a drug product is defined as: 1. A 5% change in assay from its initial value; or failure to meet the acceptance criteria for potency when using biological or immunological procedures; 2. Any degradation product's exceeding its acceptance criterion; 3. Failure to meet the acceptance criteria for appearance, physical attributes, and functionality test (e.g., color, phase separation, resuspendibility, caking, hardness, dose delivery per actuation); 4. Failure to meet the acceptance criterion for pH; or 5. Failure to meet the acceptance criteria for dissolution for 12 dosage units.
rules.exceptions: Some changes in physical attributes (e.g., softening of suppositories, melting of creams) may be expected under accelerated conditions.

rules.rule_text: Drug products packaged in impermeable containers may conduct stability studies under any controlled or ambient humidity condition since moisture or solvent loss is not a concern.
rules.source_section: Section 2.2.7.2 Drug products packaged in impermeable containers
rules.source_quote: Sensitivity to moisture or potential for solvent loss is not a concern for drug products packaged in impermeable containers that provide a permanent barrier to passage of moisture or solvent. Thus, stability studies for products stored in impermeable containers can be conducted under any controlled or ambient humidity condition.

rules.rule_text: Aqueous-based products in semi-permeable containers must be evaluated for potential water loss. Long-term studies must be at 25°C ± 2°C/40% RH ± 5% RH OR 30°C ± 2°C/35% RH ± 5% RH (12 months minimum). Accelerated at 40°C ± 2°C/NMT 25% RH (6 months minimum).
rules.source_section: Section 2.2.7.3 Drug products packaged in semi-permeable containers
rules.source_quote: Long term*: 25°C ± 2°C/40% RH ± 5% RH or 30°C ± 2°C/35% RH ± 5% RH — 12 months. Accelerated: 40°C ± 2°C/not more than (NMT) 25% RH — 6 months
rules.exceptions: Alternative approach: perform studies at higher RH and derive water loss at reference RH through calculation.

rules.rule_text: A 5% loss in water from initial value is considered a significant change for a product in a semi-permeable container after equivalent of 3 months storage at 40°C/NMT 25% RH. Exception for small containers (1 mL or less) or unit-dose products.
rules.source_section: Section 2.2.7.3 Drug products packaged in semi-permeable containers
rules.source_quote: A 5% loss in water from its initial value is considered a significant change for a product packaged in a semi-permeable container after an equivalent of 3 months' storage at 40°C/NMT 25% RH. However, for small containers (1 mL or less) or unit-dose products, a water loss of 5% or more after an equivalent of 3 months' storage at 40°C/NMT 25% RH may be appropriate, if justified.
rules.exceptions: For small containers (1 mL or less) or unit-dose products, >5% water loss may be acceptable if justified.

rules.rule_text: Drug products intended for refrigerator storage must be tested long-term at 5°C ± 3°C (12 months minimum at submission) and accelerated at 25°C ± 2°C/60% RH ± 5% RH (6 months minimum).
rules.source_section: Section 2.2.7.4 Drug products intended for storage in a refrigerator
rules.source_quote: Long term: 5°C ± 3°C — 12 months. Accelerated: 25°C ± 2°C/60% RH ± 5% RH — 6 months

rules.rule_text: Drug products intended for freezer storage must be tested long-term at -20°C ± 5°C with a minimum of 12 months data at submission. Shelf life must be based on real-time data.
rules.source_section: Section 2.2.7.5 Drug products intended for storage in a freezer
rules.source_quote: Long term: -20°C ± 5°C — 12 months. For drug products intended for storage in a freezer, the shelf life should be based on the real time data obtained at the long term storage condition.
rules.exceptions: No accelerated condition. Test at elevated temperature (e.g., 5°C ± 3°C or 25°C ± 2°C) for excursion assessment.

rules.rule_text: If the submission for drug product includes data on at least three production batches, a commitment must be made to continue the long-term studies through the proposed shelf life and the accelerated studies for 6 months.
rules.source_quote: If the submission includes data from stability studies on at least three production batches, a commitment should be made to continue the long term studies through the proposed shelf life and the accelerated studies for 6 months.
rules.exceptions: Post-approval commitment unnecessary if submission includes long-term data from three production batches covering the proposed shelf life.

rules.rule_text: Shelf life determination must use 95% one-sided confidence limit analysis. If batch-to-batch variability is small (p > 0.25 for test of slopes and intercepts), data may be combined into one overall estimate. If inappropriate to combine, shelf life must be based on the minimum time a batch can be expected to remain within acceptance criteria.
rules.source_quote: An approach for analyzing data of a quantitative attribute that is expected to change with time is to determine the time at which the 95 one-sided confidence limit for the mean curve intersects the acceptance criterion. If analysis shows that the batch-to-batch variability is small, it is advantageous to combine the data into one overall estimate. This can be done by first applying appropriate statistical tests (e.g., p values for level of significance of rejection of more than 0.25) to the slopes of the regression lines and zero time intercepts for the individual batches.

rules.rule_text: A storage statement must be established for drug product labeling based on stability evaluation. Terms such as 'ambient conditions' or 'room temperature' must be avoided. An expiration date must be displayed on the container label.
rules.source_quote: A storage statement should be established for the labeling in accordance with relevant national/regional requirements. Terms such as "ambient conditions" or "room temperature" should be avoided. There should be a direct link between the label storage statement and the demonstrated stability of the drug product. An expiration date should be displayed on the container label.

rules.rule_text: Excursions in storage conditions that exceed the defined tolerances for more than 24 hours must be described in the study report and their effect assessed.
rules.source_quote: Excursions that exceed the defined tolerances for more than 24 hours should be described in the study report and their effect assessed.
rules.exceptions: Short-term spikes due to opening of doors are accepted as unavoidable.

rules.rule_text: A pilot scale batch for solid oral dosage forms is generally, at a minimum, one-tenth of a full production scale or 100,000 tablets or capsules, whichever is the larger.
rules.source_quote: For solid oral dosage forms, a pilot scale is generally, at a minimum, one-tenth that of a full production scale or 100,000 tablets or capsules, whichever is the larger.
rules.exceptions: Whichever is the larger applies.

rules.rule_text: Photostability testing must be conducted on at least one primary batch of the drug product if appropriate. Standard conditions for photostability testing are described in ICH Q1B.
rules.source_quote: Photostability testing should be conducted on at least one primary batch of the drug product if appropriate. The standard conditions for photostability testing are described in ICH Q1B.
rules.exceptions: Applies 'if appropriate' based on dosage form and drug substance sensitivity.

rules.rule_text: Drug product stability testing must be conducted on the dosage form packaged in the container closure system proposed for marketing, including secondary packaging and container label as appropriate.
rules.source_quote: Stability testing should be conducted on the dosage form packaged in the container closure system proposed for marketing (including, as appropriate, any secondary packaging and container label).
rules.exceptions: Studies outside immediate container can form part of stress testing or supporting information.

rules.rule_text: Shelf life acceptance criteria must be derived from consideration of all available stability information. Differences between release and shelf life acceptance criteria for antimicrobial preservative content must be supported by validated correlation of chemical content and preservative effectiveness.
rules.source_quote: Shelf life acceptance criteria should be derived from consideration of all available stability information. Any differences between the release and shelf life acceptance criteria for antimicrobial preservative content should be supported by a validated correlation of chemical content and preservative effectiveness demonstrated during drug development on the product in its final formulation.

rules.rule_text: A new dosage form is defined as a drug product that is a different pharmaceutical product type but contains the same active substance as an existing approved product. Types include different routes of administration, new delivery systems, and different dosage forms of the same route.
rules.source_quote: A new dosage form is defined as a drug product which is a different pharmaceutical product type, but contains the same active substance as included in the existing drug product approved by the pertinent regulatory authority. Such pharmaceutical product types include products of different administration route (e.g., oral to parenteral), new specific functionality/delivery systems (e.g., immediate release tablet to modified release tablet) and different dosage forms of the same administration route (e.g., capsule to tablet, solution to suspension).

rules.rule_text: Stability protocols for new dosage forms must follow the parent Q1A stability guideline in principle. A reduced stability database at submission time (e.g., 6 months accelerated and 6 months long-term data from ongoing studies) may be acceptable in certain justified cases.
rules.source_quote: Stability protocols for new dosage forms should follow the guidance in the parent stability guideline in principle. However, a reduced stability database at submission time (e.g., 6 months accelerated and 6 months long term data from ongoing studies) may be acceptable in certain justified cases.
rules.exceptions: Reduction only acceptable in certain justified cases.

rules.rule_text: A reduced design (bracketing or matrixing) can be a suitable alternative to a full design when multiple design factors are involved. Any reduced design must have the ability to adequately predict the re-test period or shelf life. The potential risk of establishing a shorter re-test period or shelf life than from a full design must be assessed and justified before applying.
rules.source_quote: A reduced design can be a suitable alternative to a full design when multiple design factors are involved. Any reduced design should have the ability to adequately predict the retest period or shelf life. Before a reduced design is considered, certain assumptions should be assessed and justified. The potential risk should be considered of establishing a shorter retest period or shelf life than could be derived from a full design due to the reduced amount of data collected.

rules.rule_text: For drug substances, matrixing is of limited utility and bracketing is generally not applicable.
rules.source_quote: For the study of drug substances, matrixing is of limited utility and bracketing is generally not applicable.

rules.rule_text: A bracketing design requires that only samples at the extremes of certain design factors (e.g., strength, container size and/or fill) are tested at all time points. The use of a bracketing design is not appropriate if it cannot be demonstrated that the selected strengths or container sizes and/or fills are indeed the extremes.
rules.source_quote: As defined in the glossary to the parent guideline, bracketing is the design of a stability schedule such that only samples on the extremes of certain design factors (e.g., strength, container size and/or fill) are tested at all time points as in a full design. The use of a bracketing design would not be considered appropriate if it cannot be demonstrated that the strengths or container sizes and/or fills selected for testing are indeed the extremes.

rules.rule_text: Bracketing can be applied to multiple strengths of identical or closely related formulations without additional justification. When relative amounts of drug substance and excipients change across strengths, justification must be provided including comparable stability profiles from clinical or development batches.
rules.source_quote: Bracketing can be applied to studies with multiple strengths of identical or closely related formulations. With justification, bracketing can be applied to studies with multiple strengths where the relative amounts of drug substance and excipients change in a formulation. Such justification can include a demonstration of comparable stability profiles among the different strengths of clinical or development batches. In cases where different excipients are used among strengths, bracketing generally should not be applied.
rules.exceptions: If different excipients are used among strengths, bracketing generally should not be applied.

rules.rule_text: Bracketing can be applied to studies with the same container closure system where either container size or fill varies while the other remains constant. When both container size and fill vary, it must not be assumed that the largest and smallest containers represent the extremes of all packaging configurations.
rules.source_section: Section 2.3.1.2 Container Closure Sizes and/or Fills
rules.source_quote: Bracketing can be applied to studies of the same container closure system where either container size or fill varies while the other remains constant. However, if a bracketing design is considered where both container size and fill vary, it should not be assumed that the largest and smallest containers represent the extremes of all packaging configurations.

rules.rule_text: If, after starting bracketing studies, one of the extremes is no longer expected to be marketed, the study design can be maintained to support bracketed intermediates. A commitment must be provided to carry out stability studies on the marketed extremes post-approval.
rules.source_section: Section 2.3.2 Design Considerations and Potential Risks
rules.source_quote: If, after starting the studies, one of the extremes is no longer expected to be marketed, the study design can be maintained to support the bracketed intermediates. A commitment should be provided to carry out stability studies on the marketed extremes post-approval.

rules.rule_text: Before applying a bracketing design, its effect on re-test period or shelf life estimation must be assessed. If the stability of extremes is shown to be different, intermediates must be considered no more stable than the least stable extreme.
rules.source_section: Section 2.3.2 Design Considerations and Potential Risks
rules.source_quote: Before a bracketing design is applied, its effect on the retest period or shelf life estimation should be assessed. If the stability of the extremes is shown to be different, the intermediates should be considered no more stable than the least stable extreme (i.e., the shelf life for the intermediates should not exceed that for the least stable extreme).

rules.rule_text: Each storage condition must be treated separately under its own matrixing design. Matrixing must not be performed across test attributes. However, alternative matrixing designs for different test attributes can be applied if justified.
rules.source_quote: Each storage condition should be treated separately under its own matrixing design. Matrixing should not be performed across test attributes. However, alternative matrixing designs for different test attributes can be applied if justified.
rules.exceptions: Alternative matrixing designs for different test attributes can be applied if justified.

rules.rule_text: In a matrixing design where time points are matrixed, all selected factor combinations must be tested at the initial and final time points, while only certain fractions of designated combinations are tested at each intermediate time point. Data from at least three time points including initial must be available through the first 12 months.
rules.source_quote: In a design where time points are matrixed, all selected factor combinations should be tested at the initial and final time points, while only certain fractions of the designated combinations should be tested at each intermediate time point. In addition, data from at least three time points, including initial, should be available for each selected combination through the first 12 months of the study.

rules.rule_text: A matrixing design is applicable only if supporting data indicate predictable product stability. Matrixing is appropriate when supporting data exhibit only small variability. If supporting data show large variability, a matrixing design must not be applied.
rules.source_section: Section 2.4.4 Applicability and Degree of Reduction
rules.source_quote: In general, a matrixing design is applicable if the supporting data indicate predictable product stability. Matrixing is appropriate when the supporting data exhibit only small variability. However, where the supporting data exhibit moderate variability, a matrixing design should be statistically justified. If the supportive data show large variability, a matrixing design should not be applied.
rules.exceptions: Moderate variability requires statistical justification.

rules.rule_text: Stability data from studies in a reduced design must be treated in the same manner as data from full design studies.
rules.source_quote: Stability data from studies in a reduced design should be treated in the same manner as data from full design studies.

rules.rule_text: For matrixing at accelerated or intermediate storage conditions, testing must occur at a minimum of three time points, including initial and final, for each selected combination of factors.
rules.source_quote: For matrixing at an accelerated or intermediate storage condition, care should be taken to ensure testing occurs at a minimum of three time points, including initial and final, for each selected combination of factors.

rules.rule_text: At a minimum, the pharmaceutical development section must identify those aspects of drug substances, excipients, container closure systems, and manufacturing processes that are critical to product quality, and control strategies must be justified. Quality cannot be tested into products; it should be built in by design.
rules.source_quote: At a minimum, those aspects of drug substances, excipients, container closure systems, and manufacturing processes that are critical to product quality should be determined and control strategies justified. It is important to recognize that quality cannot be tested into products; i.e., quality should be built in by design.

rules.rule_text: The physicochemical and biological properties of the drug substance that can influence the performance of the drug product and its manufacturability must be identified and discussed. Properties to examine include solubility, water content, particle size, crystal properties, biological activity, and permeability.
rules.source_quote: The physicochemical and biological properties of the drug substance that can influence the performance of the drug product and its manufacturability, or were specifically designed into the drug substance (e.g., solid state properties), should be identified and discussed. Examples of physicochemical and biological properties that might need to be examined include solubility, water content, particle size, crystal properties, biological activity, and permeability.

rules.rule_text: The compatibility of the drug substance with excipients listed in the regulatory submission must be evaluated. For products containing more than one drug substance, compatibility of the drug substances with each other must also be evaluated.
rules.source_quote: The compatibility of the drug substance with excipients listed in 3.2.P.1 should be evaluated. For products that contain more than one drug substance, the compatibility of the drug substances with each other should also be evaluated.

rules.rule_text: Excipient ranges included in the batch formula must be justified in the Pharmaceutical Development section; this justification can be based on experience gained during development or manufacture.
rules.source_quote: Any excipient ranges included in the batch formula (3.2.P.3.2) should be justified in this section of the application; this justification can often be based on the experience gained during development or manufacture.

rules.rule_text: Use of an overage of a drug substance to compensate for degradation during manufacture or shelf life, or to extend shelf life, is discouraged. Any overages must be justified considering safety and efficacy.
rules.source_quote: In general, use of an overage of a drug substance to compensate for degradation during manufacture or a product's shelf life, or to extend shelf life, is discouraged. Any overages in the manufacture of the drug product, whether they appear in the final formulated product or not, should be justified considering the safety and efficacy of the product.

rules.rule_text: The manufacturing process development programme must identify any critical process parameters (CPPs) that should be monitored or controlled to ensure that the product is of the desired quality.
rules.source_quote: The manufacturing process development programme or process improvement programme should identify any critical process parameters that should be monitored or controlled (e.g., granulation end point) to ensure that the product is of the desired quality.

rules.rule_text: For sterile products, an appropriate method of sterilization for the drug product and primary packaging material must be chosen and the choice justified.
rules.source_quote: For those products intended to be sterile an appropriate method of sterilization for the drug product and primary packaging material should be chosen and the choice justified.

rules.rule_text: The choice and rationale for selection of the container closure system for the commercial product must be discussed. The choice of materials for primary packaging must be justified with studies demonstrating the integrity of the container and closure. Possible interaction between product and container or label must be considered.
rules.source_quote: The choice and rationale for selection of the container closure system for the commercial product (described in 3.2.P.7) should be discussed. The choice of materials for primary packaging should be justified. The discussion should describe studies performed to demonstrate the integrity of the container and closure. A possible interaction between product and container or label should be considered.

rules.rule_text: The antimicrobial preservative effectiveness must be demonstrated during development. The lowest specified concentration of antimicrobial preservative must be demonstrated to be effective by using an antimicrobial preservative effectiveness test throughout the intended shelf life.
rules.source_quote: Although chemical testing for preservative content is the attribute normally included in the drug product specification, antimicrobial preservative effectiveness should be demonstrated during development. The lowest specified concentration of antimicrobial preservative should be demonstrated to be effective in controlling micro-organisms by using an antimicrobial preservative effectiveness test.

rules.rule_text: Design space is the multidimensional combination and interaction of input variables (e.g., material attributes) and process parameters that have been demonstrated to provide assurance of quality. Working within the design space is not considered as a change. Movement out of the design space is considered to be a change and would normally initiate a regulatory post-approval change process.
rules.source_quote: Design Space: The multidimensional combination and interaction of input variables (e.g., material attributes) and process parameters that have been demonstrated to provide assurance of quality. Working within the design space is not considered as a change. Movement out of the design space is considered to be a change and would normally initiate a regulatory post approval change process. Design space is proposed by the applicant and is subject to regulatory assessment and approval.

rules.rule_text: The quality target product profile (QTPP) must be defined as the basis for the design of the product, considering route of administration, dosage form, bioavailability, strength, and stability.
rules.source_section: Section 2.1 Quality Target Product Profile (Part II Annex)
rules.source_quote: Defining the quality target product profile (QTPP) as it relates to quality, safety and efficacy, considering e.g., the route of administration, dosage form, bioavailability, strength, and stability

rules.rule_text: Critical Quality Attributes (CQAs) of the drug product must be identified so that product characteristics having an impact on product quality can be studied and controlled. A CQA is a physical, chemical, biological, or microbiological property or characteristic that should be within an appropriate limit, range, or distribution to ensure the desired product quality.
rules.source_section: Section 2.2 Critical Quality Attributes (Part II Annex)
rules.source_quote: A CQA is a physical, chemical, biological, or microbiological property or characteristic that should be within an appropriate limit, range, or distribution to ensure the desired product quality. CQAs are generally associated with the drug substance, excipients, intermediates (in-process materials) and drug product.

rules.rule_text: Risk assessment must be used as a science-based process to identify which material attributes and process parameters potentially have an effect on product CQAs. Risk assessment must be performed early in the pharmaceutical development process and repeated as more information becomes available.
rules.source_quote: Risk assessment is a valuable science-based process used in quality risk management (see ICH Q9) that can aid in identifying which material attributes and process parameters potentially have an effect on product CQAs. Risk assessment is typically performed early in the pharmaceutical development process and is repeated as more information becomes available and greater knowledge is obtained.

rules.rule_text: A design space can be described in terms of ranges of material attributes and process parameters, or through more complex mathematical relationships. Operation within the design space must result in a product meeting the defined quality.
rules.source_section: Section 2.4.2 Describing a Design Space in a Submission (Part II Annex)
rules.source_quote: A design space can be described in terms of ranges of material attributes and process parameters, or through more complex mathematical relationships. Regardless of how a design space is developed, it is expected that operation within the design space will result in a product meeting the defined quality.

rules.rule_text: A combination of proven acceptable ranges does not constitute a design space. Proven acceptable ranges based on univariate experimentation can provide useful knowledge about the process but are not equivalent to a multidimensional design space.
rules.source_section: Section 2.4.5 Design Space Versus Proven Acceptable Ranges (Part II Annex)
rules.source_quote: A combination of proven acceptable ranges does not constitute a design space. However, proven acceptable ranges based on univariate experimentation can provide useful knowledge about the process.

rules.rule_text: A control strategy must include, at a minimum, control of the critical process parameters and material attributes. The control strategy must be designed to ensure that a product of required quality will be produced consistently.
rules.source_quote: A control strategy is designed to ensure that a product of required quality will be produced consistently. The elements of the control strategy discussed in Section P.2 of the dossier should describe and justify how in-process controls and the controls of input materials (drug substance and excipients), intermediates (in-process materials), container closure system, and drug product contribute to the final product quality. These controls should be based on product, formulation and process understanding and should include, at a minimum, control of the critical process parameters and material attributes.

rules.rule_text: A Critical Process Parameter (CPP) is a process parameter whose variability has an impact on a critical quality attribute and therefore must be monitored or controlled to ensure the process produces the desired quality.
rules.source_quote: Critical Process Parameter (CPP): A process parameter whose variability has an impact on a critical quality attribute and therefore should be monitored or controlled to ensure the process produces the desired quality.

rules.rule_text: Quality by Design (QbD) is a systematic approach to development that begins with predefined objectives and emphasizes product and process understanding and process control, based on sound science and quality risk management.
rules.source_quote: Quality by Design (QbD): A systematic approach to development that begins with predefined objectives and emphasizes product and process understanding and process control, based on sound science and quality risk management.

rules.rule_text: Quality Target Product Profile (QTPP) is a prospective summary of the quality characteristics of a drug product that ideally will be achieved to ensure the desired quality, taking into account safety and efficacy of the drug product.
rules.source_quote: Quality Target Product Profile (QTPP): A prospective summary of the quality characteristics of a drug product that ideally will be achieved to ensure the desired quality, taking into account safety and efficacy of the drug product.

rules.rule_text: Changes in formulation and manufacturing processes during development and lifecycle management must be looked upon as opportunities to gain additional knowledge and support establishment of the design space.
rules.source_quote: Changes in formulation and manufacturing processes during development and lifecycle management should be looked upon as opportunities to gain additional knowledge and further support establishment of the design space.

rules.rule_text: For dosing devices (e.g., dropper pipette, pen injection device, dry powder inhaler), it must be demonstrated that a reproducible and accurate dose of the product is delivered under testing conditions that simulate patient use as far as possible.
rules.source_quote: If a dosing device is used (e.g., dropper pipette, pen injection device, dry powder inhaler), it is important to demonstrate that a reproducible and accurate dose of the product is delivered under testing conditions which, as far as possible, simulate the use of the product.

rules.rule_text: The compatibility of the drug product with reconstitution diluents (e.g., regarding precipitation, stability) must be addressed. This information must cover the recommended in-use shelf life, at the recommended storage temperature and at the likely extremes of concentration.
rules.source_quote: The compatibility of the drug product with reconstitution diluents (e.g., precipitation, stability) should be addressed to provide appropriate and supportive information for the labelling. This information should cover the recommended in-use shelf life, at the recommended storage temperature and at the likely extremes of concentration.

rules.rule_text: Established Conditions (ECs) are legally binding information considered necessary to assure product quality. Any change to ECs necessitates a submission to the regulatory authority.
rules.source_quote: ECs are legally binding information considered necessary to assure product quality. As a consequence, any change to ECs necessitates a submission to the regulatory authority.

rules.rule_text: An MAH must clearly identify the elements of CMC which they consider to be an EC and those which they consider to be supportive information. The rationales for the ECs must be provided in the appropriate CTD modules.
rules.source_section: Section 3.2.2 ECs in a Regulatory Dossier
rules.source_quote: An MAH should clearly identify the elements of CMC which they consider to be an EC and those which they consider to be supportive information. The rationales for the ECs are provided in the appropriate CTD modules.
rules.exceptions: Unless otherwise specified by regulatory requirement, identifying ECs for a given product is not mandatory.

rules.rule_text: Process parameters that need to be controlled to ensure that a product of required quality will be produced must be considered ECs. These ECs are identified through an initial risk assessment and criticality assessment that determines the level of impact a process parameter could have on product quality.
rules.source_section: Section 3.2.3.1 Identification of ECs for the Manufacturing Processes
rules.source_quote: Process parameters that need to be controlled to ensure that a product of required quality will be produced should be considered ECs. These ECs are identified through an initial risk assessment and application of knowledge gained from executed studies, prior knowledge, and a criticality assessment that determines the level of impact that a process parameter could have on product quality.

rules.rule_text: CPPs and other process parameters where an impact on product quality cannot be reasonably excluded must be identified as ECs.
rules.source_section: Section 3.2.3.1 Identification of ECs for the Manufacturing Processes
rules.source_quote: CPPs and other process parameters where an impact on product quality cannot be reasonably excluded should be identified as ECs.

rules.rule_text: Post-approval CMC change categorization must be risk-based. High-risk changes to ECs require prior approval; moderate- to low-risk changes may require only notification. Changes that are not required to be reported to regulators must only be managed and documented within the PQS.
rules.source_section: Section 2 Categorisation of Post-Approval CMC Changes
rules.source_quote: Prior approval: Certain changes are considered to have sufficient risk to require regulatory authority review and approval prior to implementation and are requested by the MAH in a suitably detailed regulatory submission. Notification: Certain moderate- to low-risk changes are judged to not require prior approval and generally require less information to support the change.

rules.rule_text: A Post-Approval Change Management Protocol (PACMP) is a regulatory tool that provides predictability and transparency regarding the requirements and studies needed to implement a change. The PACMP requires approval by the regulatory authority, and the conditions and acceptance criteria outlined in the protocol must be met before implementation.
rules.source_quote: A PACMP is a regulatory tool that provides predictability and transparency in terms of the requirements and studies needed to implement a change as the approved protocol provides an agreement between the MAH and the regulatory authority. The PACMP requires approval by the regulatory authority, and the conditions and acceptance criteria outlined in the protocol must be met and results communicated to the regulatory authority in the manner previously agreed, in order to implement the change(s).

rules.rule_text: No change outlined in a PACMP must introduce any additional risks to patient safety, product quality, or efficacy. CMC changes that would require supportive efficacy, safety (clinical or non-clinical), or human PK/PD data are not suitable for inclusion in a PACMP.
rules.source_quote: No change outlined in a PACMP should introduce any additional risks to patient safety, product quality or efficacy. A CMC change that would require supportive efficacy, safety (clinical or non-clinical), or human PK/PD data to evaluate the effect of the change (e.g., certain formulation changes, clinical or non-clinical studies to evaluate new impurities, assessment of immunogenicity/antigenicity) is not suitable for inclusion in a PACMP.

rules.rule_text: When a CMC change is to be introduced under a PACMP, the MAH is responsible for ensuring that the facility meets the GMP requirements and inspection or licensing status of the regulatory jurisdiction where the PACMP was approved.
rules.source_quote: The MAH is responsible for ensuring that whenever a CMC change is to be introduced under a PACMP, the facility meets the regulatory requirements of the regulatory jurisdiction where the PACMP was approved with respect to GMP compliance, and inspection or licensing status.

rules.rule_text: The PLCM document must serve as a central repository in the MAA for ECs and reporting categories for changes to ECs. It must include the ECs for the product, reporting categories for making changes to approved ECs, PACMPs, and post-approval CMC commitments. The PLCM document must be updated throughout the product lifecycle as needed.
rules.source_quote: The PLCM document outlines the specific plan for product lifecycle management that includes the ECs, reporting categories for changes to ECs, PACMPs (if used) and any post-approval CMC commitments. Its purpose is to encourage prospective lifecycle management planning by the MAH and to facilitate regulatory assessment and inspection. The PLCM document should be updated throughout the product lifecycle as needed.

rules.rule_text: An updated PLCM document must be included in post-approval submissions for CMC changes. The PLCM document is submitted in the original MAA or in a supplement/variation for marketed products when defining ECs. The PLCM document can be located in CTD Module 3.2.R.
rules.source_quote: The PLCM document is submitted in the original MAA or in a supplement/variation for marketed products when defining ECs. An updated PLCM document should be included in post-approval submissions for CMC changes.

rules.rule_text: An effective Pharmaceutical Quality System (PQS) as described in ICH Q10 and in compliance with regional GMP requirements is necessary across the entire supply chain and product lifecycle to support use of the lifecycle management tools described in Q12.
rules.source_quote: An effective PQS as described in ICH Q10 and in compliance with regional GMP requirements where the application is filed, is necessary across the entire supply chain and product lifecycle to support use of the tools described in this guideline.

rules.rule_text: Changes to ECs must be communicated in a timely fashion between the MAH and the regulators, and between the MAH and the manufacturing chain. A critical failure in a PQS anywhere in the supply chain may impact the ability to use Q12 tools; the company must communicate such failures to affected regulatory authorities.
rules.source_section: Section 6.2 Change Management Across the Supply Chain and Product Lifecycle
rules.source_quote: Changes to ECs should be communicated in a timely fashion between the MAH and the regulators, and between the MAH and the manufacturing chain (and vice versa). A critical failure in a PQS anywhere in the supply chain may impact the ability to use the tools in this guideline; therefore, the company should communicate such failures to affected regulatory authorities.

rules.rule_text: Stability data approaches to support post-approval CMC changes must focus on confirming the previously approved shelf-life and storage conditions, not establishing new ones. Approaches may include accelerated/stress studies, pre- and post-change comparability studies, statistical evaluation of existing stability data, and predictive degradation models.
rules.source_section: Section 9 Stability Data Approaches to Support the Evaluation of CMC Changes
rules.source_quote: Unlike the formal stability studies recommended in ICH Q1A(R2), whose objective is to establish a useful shelf-life and storage conditions for a new, yet-to-be-marketed drug substance/drug product, the purpose of stability studies, if needed, to support a post-approval CMC change is to confirm the previously approved shelf-life and storage conditions.

rules.rule_text: Where applicable, a commitment to initiate or complete ongoing, long-term stability testing on post-change batches can assure that the approved shelf life and storage conditions continue to be applicable after implementing a CMC change.
rules.source_section: Section 9 Stability Data Approaches to Support the Evaluation of CMC Changes
rules.source_quote: Where applicable, a commitment to initiate or complete ongoing, long-term stability testing on post-change batches can assure that the approved shelf life and storage conditions continue to be applicable after implementing the CMC change.

rules.rule_text: Drug substance storage conditions and shelf-life (or re-test period) are Established Conditions located in CTD section 3.2.S.7.1. Drug product storage conditions, shelf-life, and where applicable in-use storage conditions and shelf-life, are ECs located in CTD section 3.2.P.8.1.
rules.source_section: Appendix 1: CTD Sections That Contain ECs
rules.source_quote: 3.2.S.7.1 Stability Summary and Conclusions: Drug Substance storage conditions and shelf-life (or Retest period for chemicals). 3.2.P.8.1 Stability Summary and Conclusion: Drug product storage conditions and shelf-life Where applicable, in-use storage conditions and shelf-life

rules.rule_text: The ECs for analytical procedures related to the identification of ECs should include elements which assure performance of the procedure. Enhanced understanding of method parameters can lead to a wider operating window and ECs focused on method performance criteria rather than fixed conditions.
rules.source_section: Section 3.2.3.2 Identification of ECs for Analytical Procedures
rules.source_quote: Similar to the principles described for manufacturing process, ECs related to analytical procedures should include elements which assure performance of the procedure. Enhanced understanding can lead to a wider operating window that ensures method performance, where ECs can be reduced and focused on method performance (e.g., method parameters acceptable ranges rather than set points, performance criteria).

rules.rule_text: Criticality and risk of ECs must be periodically reviewed (as expected by ICH Q10) during the lifecycle of the product. ECs and reporting categories must be updated based on acquired knowledge.
rules.source_section: Section 3.2.3.1 Identification of ECs for the Manufacturing Processes
rules.source_quote: Criticality and risk should be periodically reviewed (as expected by ICH Q10) during the lifecycle of the product and the ECs and reporting categories should be updated based on acquired knowledge.

rules.rule_text: A PACMP must include a detailed description of the proposed change(s) with rationale, list of specific tests and studies to evaluate the impact, discussion of control strategy suitability, any other conditions to be met (e.g., process qualification), and the proposed reporting category.
rules.source_quote: A PACMP would typically include the following, e.g.: A detailed description of the proposed change(s), including a rationale. Based on an initial risk assessment, a list of specific tests and studies to be performed. Discussion regarding the suitability of the approved control strategy or any changes needed. Any other conditions to be met, such as confirmation that certain process qualification steps will be completed before implementation. Proposed reporting category for step 2 of the PACMP.

rules.rule_text: Drug product batch formula (qualitative and quantitative composition) is an Established Condition and must be reported if changed. Drug product container closure system (material of construction and specification, and where applicable supplier/manufacturer of primary container closure system) is also an EC.
rules.source_section: Appendix 1: CTD Sections That Contain ECs
rules.source_quote: 3.2.P.3.2 Batch Formula: Drug Product Batch Formula (Qualitative and Quantitative). 3.2.P.7 Container Closure System: Material of construction and specification. Where applicable, supplier/manufacturer of primary container closure system

rules.rule_text: The change management system must be available for review during audit/inspection. Post-implementation verification must occur to confirm relevant changes have been effective with no unintended consequences for product quality.
rules.source_quote: 12. The change management system should be available for review during audit/inspection. 10. Verifies, post-implementation, that relevant changes have been effective in achieving the desired outcome with no unintended consequences for product quality.

rules.rule_text: If significant change occurs at the accelerated storage condition between 3 and 6 months for refrigerator-stored drug substance, the proposed re-test period should be based on the real-time data available from the long-term storage condition.
rules.source_section: Section 2.1.7.2 Drug substances intended for storage in a refrigerator
rules.source_quote: If significant change occurs between 3 and 6 months' testing at the accelerated storage condition, the proposed re-test period should be based on the real time data available at the long term storage condition.

rules.rule_text: If significant change occurs within the first 3 months of accelerated testing for refrigerator-stored drug substance, a discussion must be provided addressing the effect of short-term excursions outside the label storage condition. It is considered unnecessary to continue testing through 6 months when significant change has occurred within the first 3 months.
rules.source_section: Section 2.1.7.2 Drug substances intended for storage in a refrigerator
rules.source_quote: If significant change occurs within the first 3 months' testing at the accelerated storage condition, a discussion should be provided to address the effect of short term excursions outside the label storage condition, e.g., during shipping or handling. It is considered unnecessary to continue to test a drug substance through 6 months when a significant change has occurred within the first 3 months.

rules.rule_text: Limited extrapolation of real-time data from the long-term storage condition beyond the observed range may be undertaken at approval time if justified. Justification must be based on degradation mechanism knowledge, accelerated testing results, goodness of fit of mathematical model, batch size, and existence of supporting stability data.
rules.source_quote: Limited extrapolation of the real time data from the long term storage condition beyond the observed range to extend the re-test period can be undertaken at approval time, if justified. This justification should be based on what is known about the mechanism of degradation, the results of testing under accelerated conditions, the goodness of fit of any mathematical model, batch size, existence of supporting stability data, etc.
rules.exceptions: Extrapolation assumes the same degradation relationship will continue beyond observed data.

rules.rule_text: Stability studies must include testing of those attributes of the drug substance that are susceptible to change during storage and are likely to influence quality, safety, and/or efficacy. Testing must cover physical, chemical, biological, and microbiological attributes. Validated stability-indicating analytical procedures must be applied.
rules.source_quote: Stability studies should include testing of those attributes of the drug substance that are susceptible to change during storage and are likely to influence quality, safety, and/or efficacy. The testing should cover, as appropriate, the physical, chemical, biological, and microbiological attributes. Validated stability-indicating analytical procedures should be applied.

rules.rule_text: Drug product stability studies must include testing for preservative content (e.g., antioxidant, antimicrobial preservative) and functionality tests (e.g., for a dose delivery system). Analytical procedures must be fully validated and stability-indicating.
rules.source_quote: The testing should cover, as appropriate, the physical, chemical, biological, and microbiological attributes, preservative content (e.g., antioxidant, antimicrobial preservative), and functionality tests (e.g., for a dose delivery system). Analytical procedures should be fully validated and stability indicating.

rules.rule_text: Significant differences between manufacturing processes used for pivotal clinical trial batches and the commercial process described in the regulatory submission must be discussed, including identity and use of batches produced, manufacturing site, batch size, and any significant equipment differences.
rules.source_quote: Significant differences between the manufacturing processes used to produce batches for pivotal clinical trials (safety, efficacy, bioavailability, bioequivalence) or primary stability studies and the process described in 3.2.P.3.3 should be discussed. The information should include, for example, (1) the identity (e.g., batch number) and use of the batches produced (e.g., bioequivalence study batch number), (2) the manufacturing site, (3) the batch size, and (4) any significant equipment differences.

rules.rule_text: Drug substance manufacturing site(s) (including testing) and the drug substance specification (for each quality attribute: test method and acceptance criteria) are ECs that require regulatory communication if changed.
rules.source_section: Appendix 1: CTD Sections That Contain ECs
rules.source_quote: 3.2.S.2.1 Manufacturer(s): Drug Substance Manufacturing Site(s) (including testing). 3.2.S.4.1 Specification: Drug Substance Specification. For each Quality Attribute on the specification: Test Method; Acceptance Criteria

rules.rule_text: Significant changes to the manufacturing process or controls that were not anticipated in PACMP Step 1 (e.g., change of order of unit operations) cannot be implemented as part of Step 2 and must be the subject of a regulatory submission per regional regulation or guidance.
rules.source_quote: Significant changes to the manufacturing process or controls that were not anticipated in the PACMP step 1 (e.g., change of order of unit operations) cannot be implemented as part of step 2 and should be the subject of a regulatory submission as governed by regional regulation or guidance.
rules.exceptions: Minor unanticipated modifications not affecting technical principles of the protocol are normally considered within scope if appropriately justified.

rules.rule_text: When a matrix on design factors is applied and one strength or container size and/or fill is no longer intended for marketing, stability testing of that strength or container size can be continued to support the other strengths or container sizes in the design.
rules.source_quote: When a matrix on design factors is applied, if one strength or container size and/or fill is no longer intended for marketing, stability testing of that strength or container size and/or fill can be continued to support the other strengths or container sizes and/or fills in the design.

rules.rule_text: If full shelf-life long-term data will not be available before submission for drug product after constitution or dilution, testing must be performed at 12 months or the last time point for which data will be available.
rules.source_quote: This testing should be performed on the constituted or diluted product through the proposed in-use period on primary batches as part of the formal stability studies at initial and final time points and, if full shelf life long term data will not be available before submission, at 12 months or the last time point for which data will be available.
rules.exceptions: In general, this testing need not be repeated on commitment batches.

rules.rule_text: When the design space is proposed to apply to multiple operational scales, the design space must be described in terms of relevant scale-independent parameters. The applicant must justify the relevance of a design space developed at small or pilot scale to the proposed production scale manufacturing process.
rules.source_section: Section 2.4.4 Relationship of Design Space to Scale and Equipment (Part II Annex)
rules.source_quote: If the applicant proposes the design space to be applicable to multiple operational scales, the design space should be described in terms of relevant scale-independent parameters. The applicant should justify the relevance of a design space developed at small or pilot scale to the proposed production scale manufacturing process and discuss the potential risks in the scale-up operation.

rules.rule_text: If the drug product submission does not include stability data on production batches, a commitment must be made to place the first three production batches on long-term stability studies through the proposed shelf life and on accelerated studies for 6 months.
rules.source_quote: If the submission does not include stability data on production batches, a commitment should be made to place the first three production batches on long term stability studies through the proposed shelf life and on accelerated studies for 6 months.
